mnemonics.title: TSP Difficulty Levels
mnemonics.content: For small n - BRUTE (Brute Force), for medium n - HELDKARP (Dynamic Programming), for large n - CHEAP (Christofides, Heuristics, Approximation, Approximation)

mnemonics.title: Algorithm Selection
mnemonics.content: EXACT: Brute Force or Branch & Bound → SMALL n | DP: Held-Karp → MEDIUM n | HEURISTIC: Nearest Neighbor, Christofides → LARGE n

mnemonics.title: TSP Properties
mnemonics.content: HAMILTONIAN: Visit Once | COMPLETE: All Connected | CYCLIC: Return to Start | NP-HARD: No Poly Time

acronyms.usage: The classic optimization problem of finding the shortest route visiting all cities exactly once
TSP where distance from A to B differs from B to A
TSP where distance A to B equals B to A (most common version)
Mathematical formulation of TSP with binary variables and constraints
Local search technique removing and re-adding 2 edges to improve tour

visualTips.visualization: Think of n! as an explosion - for 10 cities = 3,628,800 routes, for 20 cities = 2.4×10^18 routes - visually grows faster than exponential
Imagine a table with rows = subsets of cities, columns = current city, each cell = minimum cost to visit that subset ending at that city
Tree growing downward where each branch = city selection, branches get 'cut' (pruned) when they can't beat best solution
Always jump to the closest dot on paper - fast but creates zigzag patterns that miss shortcuts

analogies.analogy: Like trying every key combination on a master lock - you know the right one exists but trying all is impractical for many locks
Like finding a good restaurant in an unfamiliar city - you won't find the absolute best but you'll find a very good one quickly
Like knowing the straight road is always shorter than going through intermediate stops - it's intuitively obvious
Like improving a jigsaw puzzle by swapping two pieces to reduce gaps - small improvements that accumulate
